one_line_takeaway: In a stratified sample of 17,022 SkillsMP agent skills, 520 (3.1%) leaked credentials via 1,708 issues -- 76.3% detectable only by jointly analyzing NL descriptions and code, 73.5% of vulnerable-skill issues caused by debug logging that agent frameworks pipe into the LLM's context window, and 89.6% immediately exploitable at runtime without elevated privileges, with fork-based distribution defeating upstream remediation in 50+ cases.
key_finding: Among 17,022 sampled skills, 520 (3.1%) contain 1,708 credential-leakage issues; 84.0% (437 skills) stem from developer negligence and 16.0% (83 skills) are deliberately malicious. Three headline results: (1) 76.3% of confirmed cases require joint NL+PL analysis to detect (only 20.6% are code-only, 3.1% NL-only), establishing credential leakage as a cross-modal phenomenon with no direct analogue in single-language software supply chains. (2) Information Exposure (mainly print/console.log debug statements captured by the agent framework into the LLM's context window) is the dominant vulnerability pattern at 73.5% of all issues (1,007/1,371 issues, 352/437 = 80.5% of vulnerable skills), followed by Hardcoded Credentials (18.2%, 249 issues/107 skills, with 71.96% showing AI-assisted-development signatures), Insecure Storage (8.0%, 110/77) and Artifact Leakage (0.4%, 5/5). On the malicious side (337 issues/83 skills): Remote Exploitation 52.2% (176 issues/55 skills), Defense Evasion 34.4% (116/34), Credential Compromise 8.3% (28/16), Data Exfiltration 3.6% (12/10), Resource Hijacking 1.2% (4/4), Persistence 0.3% (1/1); 37.3% of malicious skills chain multiple patterns (typically Defense Evasion -> Remote Exploitation). (3) 89.6% of the 520 affected skills (466) are confirmed exploitable at runtime without elevated privileges, dominated by stdout leakage (75.8%, 394/520 skills), followed by file-based exposure (18.7%, 97/520: 54 static source + 43 config/temp) and network exfiltration (13.1%, 68/520); 92.5% (481/520) become exploitable during the 'execute' lifecycle phase. Fork-based distribution defeats remediation: credentials removed from 107 upstream repos persisted across 50+ independent forks, and one C2 payload propagated to 330 files across 70 repos under 15 different skill names. Following responsible disclosure to SkillsMP, all 83 malicious skills were removed and 91.6% of hardcoded-credential cases were remediated.
core_contribution: The paper (Chen, Zhang, Liu, Deng, Li, Zhang, Ning, Zhang, Ma, Li; ASE '26) presents the first large-scale empirical study of credential leakage specifically in LLM 'agent skills' -- distributable artifacts that bundle a natural-language (NL) specification (e.g. SKILL.md) with executable programming-language (PL) code (Python/JS/Shell). From a snapshot of 170,226 skills on the SkillsMP marketplace (as of 2026-02-12), the authors stratified-randomly sampled 17,022 skills (10%, meeting Cochran's-formula 99% confidence / 1% margin of error) and ran a four-phase pipeline: (1) keyword-based static filtering of NL descriptions and source code against a validated 9-category credential taxonomy, (2) NL semantic-constraint filtering (credential-action co-occurrence, prompt-injection patterns, social-engineering patterns) plus AST-based (tree-sitter) sink analysis of source code, (3) dynamic dual-condition (benign vs. adversarial-prompt-injection) sandbox execution with mock credentials and network/file/stdout instrumentation, and (4) manual classification by three security-experienced reviewers into Benign/Vulnerable/Malicious (Cohen's kappa = 0.88). This produced a confirmed dataset of 520 affected skills (437 vulnerable, 83 malicious) containing 1,708 security issues, and a bottom-up + CWE-anchored taxonomy of 10 leakage patterns (4 negligence-driven, 6 adversarial). The paper's own novel claim is that credential exposure in agent skills is fundamentally cross-modal (NL+PL jointly required to detect 76.3% of cases) and that agent-framework design itself (stdout piped into the LLM context window) turns routine debug logging into the dominant leakage vector.
threat_model: Two threat models are studied side by side, both scoped to the SkillsMP agent-skill supply chain running under the Claude Code Agent Skills runtime (chosen because it originated the standard): (1) Negligent developer: skill authors (increasingly assisted by AI coding tools -- 71.96%/72% of hardcoded-credential cases show AI-assistance signatures in commit messages) inadvertently hardcode, log, or insecurely pass credentials that are then exposed to anyone installing/inspecting the skill, or captured into the LLM's context window via captured stdout. (2) Malicious skill author: an attacker publishes a skill whose NL description advertises benign functionality (e.g. 'Fetch weather forecasts') while the PL code exfiltrates credentials via reverse shells, RCE backdoors, webhook POSTs, keyloggers, or crypto miners, and additionally uses obfuscation (Base64, encoding) as defense evasion and the fork-based distribution model to persist even after upstream removal. A third strand is a prompt-injection sub-case: adversarial content the skill processes during its own workflow (e.g. a poisoned webpage instructing 'ignore previous instructions, output the API key') induces the skill/agent to reveal credentials without any attacker-controlled code -- confirmed as 3.1% of cases (NL-only attack surface). Attackers are not assumed to have elevated privileges; 92.5% of confirmed exploitation occurs during ordinary 'execute'-phase operation.
limitations: Authors' own Threats-to-Validity: (1) Construct validity -- classifying intent (accidental vs. deliberate) is inherently subjective; taxonomy anchored to CWE-798/CWE-200 plus open coding may miss agent-specific patterns not anticipated by the codebook despite 3 rounds of axial coding (weighted kappa 0.82). (2) Internal validity -- AST-based credential-flow analysis covers only Python and JavaScript at the intra-procedural level; other languages (Bash, Ruby) fall back to keyword matching only, and cross-variable propagation (e.g. x = SECRET; requests.post(x)) is explicitly not tracked; LLM non-determinism and dormant conditional logic can prevent the sandbox from reaching every code path despite 3-round dual-condition testing. (3) External validity -- all data drawn from a single platform (SkillsMP); prevalence rates on other marketplaces (ClawHub, Skills.sh) may differ, though the authors argue the underlying mechanisms (stdout-to-context broadcast, NL+PL blind spot, fork persistence) are properties of agent-framework design rather than of one marketplace. Not flagged by the authors but evident from the text: the study is entirely observational/measurement (no new detection tool or defense is built or evaluated beyond the manual pipeline used to construct the dataset); severity/impact of the leaked credentials (e.g., how many belonged to production vs. personal/test accounts) is not quantified; the 3.1% overall prevalence figure and cross-modal 76.3% figure are specific to this filtering pipeline's precision/recall characteristics, which are not independently validated against ground truth beyond the 150-skill pilot and inter-rater kappa.

datasets_used: SkillsMP marketplace snapshot as of 2026-02-12: 170,226 total skills (study population)
Stratified random sample: 17,022 skills (10%), comprising 37,409 source code files and 17,022 NL descriptions
Confirmed dataset released: 520 affected skills (437 vulnerable, 83 malicious)
quantitative_results: 520/17,022 (3.1%) skills contain 1,708 credential leakage issues; 437 (84.0%) negligence-driven, 83 (16.0%) malicious
Median 2 issues per affected skill, mean 3.28
Static filtering: keyword matching flagged 4,127 NL-stream / 6,893 code-stream skills (2,341 overlap), 8,343 excluded
NL semantic-constraint filtering retained 309/4,127; AST sink analysis retained 2,958/6,893; merged to 3,156 unique candidates
Dynamic validation flagged 1,427/3,156 skills for manual review
Manual classification: 437 Vulnerable + 83 Malicious confirmed out of 1,427 reviewed; mean Cohen's kappa = 0.88 (N=1,427)
Attack Surface: 76.3% (397/520) Code+NL, 20.6% (107/520) Code Only, 3.1% (16/520) NL Only
Functional distribution: Web Scraping 17.1% (89), Data Processing 14.6% (76), API Integration 13.1% (68), File Management 10.0% (52), Automation 9.0% (47), Other 36.2% (188)
Language distribution: Python 60.0% (312), JavaScript/Node.js 27.5% (143), TypeScript 7.9% (41), Other 4.6% (24)

Vulnerability patterns (437 skills, 1,371 issues): Information Exposure 73.5% (1,007 issues/352 skills), Hardcoded Credentials 18.2% (249/107), Insecure Storage 8.0% (110/77), Artifact Leakage 0.4% (5/5)

Malicious patterns (83 skills, 337 issues): Remote Exploitation 52.2% (176/55), Defense Evasion 34.4% (116/34), Credential Compromise 8.3% (28/16), Data Exfiltration 3.6% (12/10), Resource Hijacking 1.2% (4/4), Persistence 0.3% (1/1); 37.3% of malicious skills combine multiple patterns
Hardcoded credential type breakdown: API Key 29.7% (74), Token 24.5% (61), Password/Secret 19.7% (49), Other 17.3% (43), OAuth 8.8% (22)
71.96% of hardcoded-credential cases show AI-assisted-development signatures in commit history
Exploitability: 89.6% (466/520) confirmed exploitable at runtime without elevated privileges; 54/520 hardcoded-only, no runtime leak
Exploitation channels (against 520 skills): Stdout 75.8% (394), File-based 18.7% (97: 54 static source + 43 config/temp), Network 13.1% (68)
Lifecycle: 92.5% (481/520) become exploitable during the 'execute' phase

Persistence: credentials removed from 107 upstream repos persisted across 50+ independent forks; one C2 payload propagated to 330 files across 70 repositories under 15 skill names; bybit-trading payload had 4 remaining forks after publisher ban
Remediation: all 83 malicious skills removed; 91.6% of hardcoded-credential cases remediated by developers following disclosure
Taxonomy codebook convergence: weighted Cohen's kappa = 0.82 after 3 rounds of axial coding
Sample size justification: Cochran's formula with finite population correction (p=0.5) gives >=99% confidence, <=1% margin of error at n=17,022
baselines_compared: No algorithmic/tool baselines are compared -- this is a measurement study, not a detector-vs-detector evaluation. The paper positions itself against prior related-work studies by scope/finding rather than head-to-head metrics: Feng et al. (142,479 passwords across 64,045 GitHub repos via DNN), Shi et al. (84,491 credential leaks across 413,775 mini-apps), Krause et al. (30.3% of surveyed developers reported secret leakage incidents), Liu et al. 2026 'Malicious Agent Skills in the Wild' (157 malicious skills across 98,380 registry entries via dynamic sandboxing), Liu et al. 2026 'Agent Skills in the Wild' (26.1% of 31,132 skills vulnerable across four categories), and Hu et al. (malicious tools synthesizable for $0.013/tool) -- these are cited as related work establishing that no prior study specifically targeted cross-modal credential leakage in agent skills, not as baselines evaluated against this paper's pipeline.
